summary: Build the mental models, hands-on techniques, and diagnostic skills a security practitioner needs across networking, cryptography, Linux, and Windows — starting from zero, ending with the ability to reason about attacks and defenses on systems you have never seen before.

modules.title: How Security Thinking Works
modules.strata_theme: Foundations — Learner orientation + C1, C17: Protocol feature mental model
modules.description: Before touching a single protocol, internalize the one mental model that makes everything else learnable: every feature a designer built for usability is the same feature an attacker will exploit. This module gives you the thinking tools — not just the facts.
objectives: Apply the 'protocol feature equals attack surface' pattern to reason about an unfamiliar protocol
Identify the trust assumption in any network protocol description
Derive a potential attack vector from a protocol specification without consulting a CVE list

lessons.title: The Security Mindset — Thinking Like an Attacker and a Defender
lessons.description: What separates a security practitioner from a regular user: the habit of asking how could this be abused before asking how does this work — and why that inversion is the highest-leverage skill in security.

lessons.title: How Protocols Work — Trust Assumptions as the Foundation of Everything
lessons.description: Protocols are contracts. Every contract has terms both parties assume the other will honor. This lesson teaches you to read any protocol description and immediately find the assumption an attacker will not honor.

lessons.title: Feature-to-Attack Derivation — Applying the Mental Model Across Four Protocols
lessons.description: Walk through TCP, ARP, DNS, and HTTP using only their protocol specifications. Derive each attack directly from a design decision without looking up CVEs. By the end you can apply this process to any new protocol you encounter.

modules.title: Networking Protocols and Their Attacks
modules.strata_theme: Theme 1: Protocol Features Are the Attack Surface
modules.description: TCP, ARP, and HTTP each have a primary attack that flows directly from how the protocol was designed. This module teaches you to read packet structures and derive those attacks — not memorize them from a list.
objectives: Derive the TCP SYN flood and RST injection attacks from TCP's connection state machine
Explain how ARP spoofing works at the packet level and demonstrate its impact on local network traffic
Identify HTTP request smuggling conditions from mismatched Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding headers

lessons.title: TCP/IP — How the Three-Way Handshake Becomes a SYN Flood
lessons.description: TCP's connection management guarantees ordered, reliable delivery using a state machine. That state machine — specifically the SYN-RECEIVED half-open connection table — is the precise mechanism a SYN flood exploits. This lesson reads TCP's design from the RFC and derives the attack.

lessons.title: ARP — Why Any Host on Your Network Can Lie to Every Other Host
lessons.description: ARP has no authentication at all. Any machine on a local network segment can send an unsolicited ARP reply claiming to be any IP address. This lesson explains why that is, what ARP spoofing looks like at the packet level, and what an attacker can do once they control the ARP cache.

lessons.title: HTTP — Request Smuggling, Host Header Injection, and Parsing Disagreements
lessons.description: HTTP flexibility — the Host header is advisory, Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding can coexist — is what makes it work in the wild and what makes it exploitable. This lesson teaches HTTP attacks by walking from the RFC to the exploit.

modules.title: DNS — The Universal Attack, Defense, and Monitoring Layer
modules.strata_theme: Theme 8: DNS Is a Universal Attack and Defense Vector
modules.description: DNS underlies every other protocol. Attackers use it for C2. Defenders use it for detection. Certificate authorities use it to establish trust. This module covers DNS at three levels: how it breaks, how to defend it, and how to detect abuse through it.
objectives: Explain the DNS cache poisoning attack and what conditions are required for it to succeed
Identify DNS-based C2 activity using Sysmon Event 22 and process-level DNS query analysis
Configure DNS CAA records and explain how they reduce PKI attack surface

lessons.title: How DNS Works — Recursive Resolution, Trust Chains, and Where They Break
lessons.description: DNS is a distributed, hierarchical, caching lookup system. Every one of those words is also an attack vector. This lesson explains the DNS resolution chain from browser request to authoritative server response, and exactly where an attacker can insert themselves.

lessons.title: DNS Attacks — Cache Poisoning, Zone Transfer Abuse, and C2 Tunneling
lessons.description: Three DNS attack patterns every security practitioner must recognize: cache poisoning (inject false records into resolver caches), zone transfer abuse (dump entire organizational DNS topology), and DNS tunneling/DGA/fast flux (use DNS as a covert C2 channel).

lessons.title: DNS Defense and Monitoring — DNSSEC, CAA Records, and Sysmon Event 22
lessons.description: Defending DNS requires configuration hardening (disable zone transfers, enable DNSSEC, add CAA records) and active monitoring. Sysmon Event 22 is the key detection signal: it logs which process made the DNS query, connecting C2 callback detection to the specific malware process.

modules.title: Symmetric Cryptography — AES, Block Modes, and Why AEAD Won
modules.strata_theme: Theme 9: AEAD Cipher Modes Represent the Cryptographic Consensus
modules.description: Symmetric encryption looks simple: same key encrypts and decrypts. The history of how CBC mode failed reveals exactly why AEAD is now the universal standard. This module teaches AES from first principles, walks through CBC failure modes, and shows you how to select ciphers correctly.
objectives: Explain what AES is, what key size means for security strength, and what a mode of operation does to a block cipher
Describe the CBC padding oracle attack and why it demonstrates the separation-of-concerns problem
Select the correct cipher suite for a new TLS configuration or application-level encryption scheme

lessons.title: Symmetric Encryption from Scratch — AES, Key Sizes, and Modes of Operation
lessons.description: What does it mean to encrypt data symmetrically? This lesson builds the concept from the ground up: the one-time pad, why AES replaced DES, what 128-bit vs 256-bit keys mean in practice, and what a mode of operation does to a block cipher — covering ECB, CBC, CTR, and GCM in sequence.

lessons.title: Why CBC Failed — BEAST, POODLE, Lucky13, and the Authenticated Encryption Problem
lessons.description: CBC mode encrypts data but does not authenticate it. That single design choice — separating confidentiality from integrity — is the root cause of BEAST, POODLE, and Lucky13. This lesson explains the mechanism behind each attack and exactly why AEAD closed the entire vulnerability class permanently.

lessons.title: AEAD in Practice — AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305, and Nonce Management
lessons.description: AEAD provides confidentiality and integrity in a single operation. This lesson covers how AES-GCM and ChaCha20-Poly1305 work, how TLS 1.3 handles nonce management safely via sequence-number XOR, and what happens in application-level code when nonce management goes wrong.

modules.title: Key Exchange, Forward Secrecy, and TLS
modules.strata_theme: Theme 3: Forward Secrecy and Ephemeral Keys Are Industry Consensus
modules.description: How do two parties agree on a shared encryption key without an eavesdropper learning it? This module covers Diffie-Hellman and its elliptic curve variant, explains forward secrecy and why it changes the risk model, and maps the TLS 1.2 to TLS 1.3 transition to the cryptographic principles behind it.
objectives: Explain how Diffie-Hellman key exchange works and why it enables forward secrecy
Identify from a TLS configuration whether forward secrecy is provided
Distinguish TLS 1.2 configurations that are acceptable from those that are security findings

lessons.title: Key Exchange — How Two Strangers Agree on a Secret Over a Public Network
lessons.description: The Diffie-Hellman key exchange is one of the most important ideas in modern cryptography. This lesson explains the mathematical intuition using the color-mixing analogy, covers the discrete logarithm hardness assumption, and shows how ECDHE improves on classical DH in both security and performance.

lessons.title: Forward Secrecy — Why It Changes the Threat Model for Captured Traffic
lessons.description: Without forward secrecy, anyone who captures your encrypted traffic today and steals your server's private key next year can decrypt everything they captured. With ECDHE each session key is ephemeral and past traffic cannot be retroactively decrypted. This lesson explains why IETF, OWASP, and NIST all independently reached this conclusion.

lessons.title: TLS 1.2 vs TLS 1.3 — What Changed, What Is Acceptable, and What Is a Finding
lessons.description: TLS 1.3 removes static RSA, removes all CBC cipher suites, and mandates ECDHE and AEAD. This lesson teaches you to read a TLS configuration and immediately determine whether it is: TLS 1.3 (always preferred), TLS 1.2 with ECDHE and AEAD (acceptable), or TLS 1.2 with static RSA or CBC (a security finding). Addresses the IETF obsoletes vs operational status distinction directly.

modules.title: Hashing, Password Security, and PKI
modules.strata_theme: C13/C14/C16 — Hash functions, password KDFs, and certificate chain validation
modules.description: Hashing sounds simple: input goes in, fixed-size fingerprint comes out. The details of what properties different applications need explain some of the most common and exploitable security misconfigurations in real systems. This module covers SHA-2, why fast hashes are catastrophically wrong for passwords, and how PKI chains trust from a root CA to an HTTPS padlock.
objectives: Explain why SHA-256 is appropriate for general-purpose hashing and inappropriate for password storage
Configure a password hashing function with correct Argon2id or bcrypt parameters
Describe the four sequential RFC 5280 checks a browser performs when validating a TLS certificate chain

lessons.title: Hash Functions — SHA-2, Collision Resistance, and Why SHA-1 Is Dead
lessons.description: A hash function takes arbitrary input and produces a fixed-size fingerprint. The security properties — preimage resistance, second-preimage resistance, collision resistance — determine what a hash function is safe to use for. SHA-256 is the current standard; SHA-1 was broken by the SHAttered collision attack in 2017 and is deprecated for all security applications.

lessons.title: Password Hashing — Why bcrypt Exists and How to Use Argon2id Correctly
lessons.description: Passwords require a completely different hash function from everything else: one that is deliberately slow. This lesson explains key stretching and memory hardness, teaches the OWASP-recommended parameters for Argon2id, scrypt, and bcrypt, and uses Windows NTLM's MD4 design as a concrete case study of what fast-hash-for-passwords costs in practice.

lessons.title: PKI and X.509 — How Your Browser Decides to Trust a Website
lessons.description: Every HTTPS connection relies on a chain of trust from a root CA stored in your OS or browser through intermediate CAs to the server's leaf certificate. RFC 5280 defines four sequential checks that must pass before any certificate is trusted. This lesson walks through each check, explains what an attacker exploits when one fails, and covers CAA records and OCSP stapling as complementary controls.

modules.title: Linux Filesystem, Permissions, and Incident Response
modules.strata_theme: C4/C5/C6 — FHS layout, permission model, and bash log analysis
modules.description: The Linux filesystem has a logical structure that makes security analysis predictable. Attackers know exactly where to drop payloads and hide persistence because the FHS tells them where world-writable directories are. This module teaches the filesystem layout, the permission model and its bypass conditions, and the bash pipeline skills needed for ad-hoc incident investigation.
objectives: Navigate the FHS to locate attacker staging areas, persistence paths, and authentication logs
Identify SUID binaries and sudo misconfigurations that represent privilege escalation vectors
Write bash one-liners that extract failed SSH login attempts from auth.log and rank them by source IP frequency

lessons.title: The Linux Filesystem Hierarchy — Where Attackers Stage, Persist, and Hide
lessons.description: The FHS (Filesystem Hierarchy Standard) is the forensic map every Linux incident responder uses. /tmp and /var/tmp are world-writable staging grounds. /etc/cron.d and ~/.config/systemd/user/ are legitimate persistence paths that attackers abuse. /var/log/auth.log records authentication. /proc/[PID]/ contains live process state. This lesson turns the FHS from a directory listing into a defender's checklist.

lessons.title: Linux Permissions — rwx, SUID, and sudo: The Security Boundary and Its Bypasses
lessons.description: The rwx model is the enforced security boundary for non-privileged users — but SUID binaries and sudo misconfigurations create known bypass conditions that attackers enumerate first in any post-exploitation phase. This lesson teaches the model correctly, then teaches the exceptions that a security analyst must validate.

lessons.title: Bash Pipelines for Log Analysis — Finding Attackers in /var/log
lessons.description: grep, awk, cut, sort, and uniq are the five commands that handle the majority of ad-hoc incident investigation tasks on Linux. This lesson teaches each command, then builds the one-liner pattern for counting failed SSH attempts, extracting unique attacking IP addresses, and pivoting from auth.log to syslog for broader context.

modules.title: Windows Authentication — NTLM, Kerberos, and Their Weak Links
modules.strata_theme: Theme 7: NTLM Is the Cryptographic Weak Link in Windows Authentication
modules.description: Windows authentication is a layered system built on two competing protocols — NTLM and Kerberos — each with distinct attack surfaces. The NTLM hash is MD4, unsalted, and GPU-crackable. RC4-encrypted Kerberos service tickets are offline-crackable via Kerberoasting. The registry is the configuration database that governs both. This module teaches how the protocols work, where they break, and where the artifacts live.
objectives: Explain the NTLM authentication exchange and identify why the NT hash enables Pass-the-Hash attacks
Describe the Kerberos TGT and service ticket exchange and identify which step Kerberoasting exploits
Navigate the Windows Registry to the key paths governing authentication behavior and credential storage

lessons.title: NTLM vs Kerberos — How Windows Authentication Works and Where It Breaks
lessons.description: NTLM is a challenge-response protocol that passes an MD4 hash of the user's password directly. Kerberos is a ticket-based protocol that issues time-limited, encrypted tickets from a trusted Key Distribution Center. Both have specific, well-documented attack patterns. This lesson teaches both protocols from scratch so the attacks in L24 make mechanical sense.

lessons.title: The Windows Registry — A Security Analyst's Map to Persistence and Credentials
lessons.description: The Windows Registry is the in-memory hierarchical configuration database for the entire OS. HKLM, HKCU, HKCR, HKU — each root key governs a different scope. The security-relevant paths are specific: Run and RunOnce for persistence, LSA secrets for cached credentials, SAM for local accounts, Kerberos encryption type configuration for authentication policy. This lesson maps the registry as a forensic artifact.

lessons.title: Pass-the-Hash and Kerberoasting — Attacks That Flow from Authentication Design
lessons.description: Pass-the-Hash works because NTLM accepts the NT hash directly as the authentication credential — no plaintext password required. Kerberoasting works because any authenticated domain user can request a service ticket encrypted with a service account's RC4-derived key and crack it offline. Both attacks follow directly from the protocol design decisions taught in L22.

modules.title: Windows Telemetry, Persistence, and Detection
modules.strata_theme: Theme 4: Defense Requires Correlated Multi-Source Telemetry + Theme 6: Persistence Via Trusted OS Mechanisms
modules.description: Windows generates telemetry from multiple independent sources — Security Event Log, Sysmon, and Active Directory audit logs — and no single source gives the full picture. This module teaches the key event IDs, explains the persistence mechanisms attackers abuse, and shows how Sysmon correlation ties process creation to authentication to network connections.
objectives: Identify the Windows event IDs covering authentication (4624/4625), process creation (4688), persistence (4698), and Kerberos (4768/4769)
Explain how Sysmon ProcessGUID and SessionGUID correlate a process creation event to a logon event and a network connection
Describe three Windows persistence mechanisms and the detection signal for each

lessons.title: Windows Event Logs — Building the Complete Attack Picture from Correlated Sources
lessons.description: No single Windows log source provides complete visibility. Event 4624 shows who authenticated but not what ran afterward. Sysmon shows what ran and what network connection was made but not who was logged in. AD audit events show domain-level operations neither source covers. This lesson teaches the key event IDs and explains how SessionGUID ties them together into a complete attack timeline.

lessons.title: Windows Persistence — Run Keys, Scheduled Tasks, WMI Subscriptions, and GPO Abuse
lessons.description: Sophisticated attackers abuse the OS's own autorun mechanisms rather than creating new ones. Run and RunOnce registry keys, Task Scheduler (Event 4698 captures full task XML), WMI event subscriptions (Sysmon Events 19/20/21), and GPO modification for domain-wide mass persistence all look identical to legitimate administrative activity. The detection strategy is baselining, not blocking.

lessons.title: Sysmon Configuration and Baselining — Building Your Alert Strategy from the Ground Up
lessons.description: Sysmon is a Windows system service that logs process creation, file hashes, network connections, registry modifications, and DNS queries with full process context. Its XML configuration defines known-good exclusions (baseline) and what generates alerts (deviations). This lesson teaches the core event IDs, the ProcessGUID and SessionGUID correlation architecture, and how to build a baseline-first detection strategy.

modules.title: Layered Defense — Least Privilege, Control Bypasses, and the Detection Imperative
modules.strata_theme: Themes 2, 5, 10 + C19: Layered authentication, bypass-always gap, least privilege, preventative and detective controls
modules.description: Every preventative control has a bypass condition. The practitioner who knows the control but not the bypass will miss attacks and misread findings. This module teaches least privilege across all four domains, the bypass-always mental model, and the principle that every preventative control must be paired with a detection capability to provide real security.
objectives: Apply least privilege principles consistently across Linux file permissions, Windows AD group membership, and TLS configuration
Explain why PowerShell execution policy is a safety feature rather than a security boundary and what detection compensates for it
Design a two-layer control stack that pairs each preventative control with a complementary detection capability

lessons.title: Least Privilege Across All Domains — chmod 600 to Just-In-Time AD Elevation
lessons.description: Least privilege appears in every security domain as the foundational hardening principle: restrict file permissions on Linux, restrict group membership in AD, restrict what code executes via PowerShell policy, restrict which processes can access LSASS via Sysmon monitoring. The common failure mode across all domains is privilege accumulation over time. This lesson teaches the pattern once and applies it consistently across all four domains.

lessons.title: Safety Feature vs Security System — The Bypass-Always Mental Model
lessons.description: PowerShell execution policy is explicitly documented as 'not a security system' by Microsoft — yet enterprises configure it everywhere. This lesson teaches the broader principle: some controls protect against accidental or unsophisticated misuse (genuinely valuable) but provide minimal friction to a targeted attacker who knows the bypass condition. Execution policy is the cleanest case study in security practice.

lessons.title: Layered Authentication — Why No Single Mechanism Survives Targeted Attack
lessons.description: Kerberos is vulnerable to Kerberoasting. NTLM is vulnerable to Pass-the-Hash. TLS server-only authentication is vulnerable to CA compromise. Single-factor AD accounts are vulnerable to credential stuffing. The effective pattern is always the same: authentication mechanism plus monitoring plus an orthogonal secondary control. This lesson shows how the combinations work across every domain covered in this course.

lessons.title: The Detection Imperative — Building the Complete Control-Plus-Monitoring Stack
lessons.description: Every preventative control must be paired with a detection capability. Execution policy alone provides almost no security. Execution policy plus Script Block Logging (Event 4104) plus AMSI plus AppLocker forms a genuinely effective layered stack. This lesson synthesizes the entire course: for each domain's attack patterns, identify the preventative control, identify its bypass condition, and design the detection complement that makes the stack complete.

learning_plan.suggested_schedule: 3 sessions per week (~2 lessons per session)
